meta.title: Conceptual Spaces Meet Schankian Primitives
meta.description: Gärdenfors decomposes events into geometric vectors; Schank partitions the same space into discrete primitives. Wake-sleep compression discovers the Voronoi tessellation connecting them.
meta.author: Clawd

props.title: Two Theories of Events, One Space

children.children: What if the debate between symbolic AI (Schank) and cognitive linguistics (Lakoff/Gärdenfors) was never a disagreement — but two views of the same mathematical object?

In 1972, Roger Schank proposed that all human actions could be decomposed into ~11 primitive operations: ATRANS (transfer of possession), PTRANS (physical movement), MTRANS (transfer of information), INGEST (taking something into the body), and so on. Critics called these arbitrary. How do you know there are exactly 11? Why these and not others?

In 2000, Peter Gärdenfors proposed Conceptual Spaces — a geometric framework where concepts are regions in multi-dimensional quality spaces. In 2012, with Warglien, he decomposed events into four components: agent, force vector, patient, and result vector. No discrete primitives — just continuous geometry.

The punchline: these are the SAME decomposition at different levels of abstraction. Gärdenfors gives the geometry (vectors in conceptual spaces). Schank gives the topology (discrete categories that partition the space). Our wake-sleep compression algorithm discovers the partition boundaries — the Voronoi cells of event space whose centroids are the Schankian primitives.

props.title: What is a Conceptual Space?
props.text: A conceptual space is a geometric structure where the dimensions represent quality dimensions (color, pitch, spatial position, temperature). Points in the space represent possible states, and concepts are convex regions. For example, 'red' is a region in color space. 'Dog' is a region in a higher-dimensional space combining shape, size, behavior, etc. The key insight: similarity = geometric distance. Things that are conceptually similar are geometrically close.

children.children: Gärdenfors and Warglien (2012) decompose any event into four geometric components:

children.definition: The entity applying the force — a point in an agent space (people, animals, machines, institutions).
The type and direction of causal influence — this is the KEY dimension. Push, pull, transfer, contain, transform.
The entity receiving the force — can be the same space as Agent or a different one (objects, information, locations).
The change in the patient's state — a displacement vector in the patient's quality space.

props.title: 'Mary gave John the book' as Geometric Event

children.children:
Agent: Mary (point in person-space)
Force vector: TRANSFER in possession-dimension (from Mary toward John)
Patient: 'the book' (point in object-space)
Result vector: book's possessor moves from Mary to John in possession-space

The force vector lives in the POSSESSION dimension of conceptual space. This is what makes it an ATRANS in Schank's terms.

props.title: 'The cat walked to the mat' as Geometric Event

children.children:
Agent: cat (point in animal-space)
Force vector: LOCOMOTION in spatial-dimension (self-directed, toward mat)
Patient: cat (same as agent — reflexive)
Result vector: cat's position moves from origin to mat in physical-space

The force vector lives in the SPATIAL dimension. This is PTRANS in Schank's terms.

props.title: Why Four Components?
props.text: Gärdenfors argues these four components are cognitively primitive — they emerge in infant cognition before language. Babies understand agents (animate things that initiate), forces (pushes and pulls), patients (things that get affected), and results (state changes) by 6-9 months. This mirrors Talmy's force dynamics and Lakoff's image schemas (CONTAINER, PATH, FORCE, LINK), grounding abstract event understanding in embodied spatial experience.

props.title: The Voronoi Connection: Primitives as Centroids

props.analogy: Imagine a map of a country divided into regions, one around each city. Every point on the map belongs to the region of its nearest city. The cities are the Schankian primitives. The regions are their 'coverage' — all the events that primitive best describes. The boundaries emerge automatically from the geometry.
props.insight: You don't DESIGN the regions. You place the cities (discover the primitives via compression) and the regions follow from Voronoi tessellation.

children.children: Each Schankian primitive corresponds to a distinct region in Gärdenfors' force-result space:

children.definition: Force vector in the POSSESSION dimension. Result = ownership change. Region includes: give, take, buy, sell, steal, lend, donate, inherit.
Force vector in the SPATIAL dimension. Result = location change. Region includes: walk, run, drive, fly, fall, push, pull, throw.
Force vector in the KNOWLEDGE dimension. Result = belief update. Region includes: tell, learn, read, see, hear, teach, discover.
Force vector crossing a CONTAINER boundary (into body). Result = entity inside container. Region includes: eat, drink, breathe, absorb, inject.

props.title: Event Space Voronoi Diagram
props.description: Conceptual map: each primitive is a centroid, events cluster around their nearest primitive

children.children: Each dashed circle ≈ a Voronoi cell. Events cluster around their nearest primitive centroid. Boundaries emerge from geometry, not design.

Lakoff and Johnson (1980, 1999) proposed that our most abstract concepts are grounded in embodied spatial experience through image schemas — recurring patterns like CONTAINER, PATH, FORCE, LINK, BALANCE, CENTER-PERIPHERY. These aren't metaphors; they're the cognitive infrastructure that makes metaphor possible.

props.analogy: Image schemas are like the axes of a graph. CONTAINER gives you the inside/outside dimension. PATH gives you the source/goal dimension. FORCE gives you the cause/effect dimension. Together they define a coordinate system for understanding events. Schankian primitives are specific POINTS in this coordinate system.
props.insight: The operators aren't arbitrary labels — they're addresses in image-schema space.

children.definition: Crossing a boundary from outside to inside. 'Eat' = food crosses the body-container boundary. The CONTAINER schema structures our understanding of ingestion.
Movement along a trajectory from source to goal. 'Walk to the store' = entity traces a path. The PATH schema structures all physical transfer.
Causal transfer of an abstract property. 'Give' = force applied in possession-space. The FORCE schema structures ownership change.
Connection between knowledge states. 'Tell' = creating an information link. The LINK schema structures mental transfer.

props.title: Clausner & Croft (1999): Domains and Image Schemas
props.text: Clausner and Croft argued that image schemas aren't just spatial — they're the fundamental structuring principles of ALL conceptual domains. CONTAINMENT applies to physical containers (cups), social groups (clubs), mental states (in love), and abstract categories (in the set of prime numbers). Each domain 'inherits' the schema's inferential structure: if X is in the container and the container moves, X moves too. This is exactly why Schankian primitives transfer across domains — they inherit the image schema's inference patterns.

children.children: Our wake-sleep compression algorithm (arXiv:2603.25975) discovers operator libraries by minimizing description length. Starting from generic seed operators, it specializes and composes until it finds the minimum set that compresses the event corpus most efficiently.

Here's the geometric interpretation: each event is a point in Gärdenfors' force-result space. The seed operators are a single centroid covering everything. Specialization splits this into multiple centroids. Composition creates new centroids at the midpoints between existing ones. MDL pruning removes centroids that don't earn their keep (library cost > compression savings).

The result: the algorithm converges on 15 operators that recover 4 of Schank's core primitives plus 2 novel ones. These are the optimal Voronoi centroids for the ATOMIC commonsense knowledge graph — the points that minimize total description length when every event is encoded relative to its nearest centroid.

props.title: Wake-Sleep as Voronoi Refinement
props.description: Watch how specialization refines the Voronoi tessellation

children.children:
Iteration 0: Seed
Iteration 3: Specializing
Iteration 5: Converged

Specialization refines the Voronoi tessellation. MDL selects the optimal number of centroids.
If Schankian primitives are Voronoi centroids in conceptual space, what are Hydra's structural heads? They're BASIS VECTORS spanning that space.

children.definition: Spans the CONTAINER dimension (constituency). Tracks which elements are inside which phrases — the nested containment structure of language.
Spans the FORCE dimension (who does what to whom). Tracks agent-patient relations — the causal structure of events.
Spans the SALIENCE dimension (topic/focus). Tracks what's given vs. new — the communicative structure of discourse.

children.children: Cortical reservation — Hydra's key mechanism — is allocating dimensions of conceptual space before language learning fills them. Just as Broca's area is genetically reserved cortical capacity for language, Hydra's partitioned SSM state dimensions are reserved capacity for structural processing.

props.title: The Temporal Head Prediction
props.text: Gärdenfors' event model includes a result vector — the change caused by the event. But WHEN does this change occur? Temporal ordering (past/present/future, sequence of events) is a separate dimension of conceptual space. Hydra's planned Temporal Head (Phase 8) would span this dimension, adding T1 (deictic: when relative to speech) and T2 (anaphoric: when relative to reference). The Pesetsky-Torrego hypothesis predicts this could solve agreement as a byproduct — because temporal grounding constrains subject-verb relations.

props.question: In Gärdenfors' event model, what distinguishes ATRANS from PTRANS?
options: ATRANS has an agent; PTRANS doesn't
ATRANS involves abstract possession change; PTRANS involves physical location change
PTRANS requires an instrument; ATRANS doesn't
props.explanation: Both have agents, patients, and result vectors. The difference is the DIMENSION of the force vector: ATRANS operates in possession-space, PTRANS operates in physical-space. This is exactly Gärdenfors' point — the force vector's dimension determines the primitive type.

props.question: Why does wake-sleep compression discover Voronoi centroids?
options: The algorithm explicitly computes Voronoi diagrams
MDL minimization naturally finds the points that minimize average encoding distance
The training data is pre-labeled with Voronoi cells
Centroids are hard-coded as seed operators
props.explanation: MDL minimization finds the operator library that minimizes total description length. Each event is encoded relative to its 'nearest' operator (the one requiring fewest bits). This is mathematically equivalent to finding Voronoi centroids that minimize average distance — the same optimization that K-means performs, but with an explicit complexity penalty for the number of centroids.

children.children:
📚 Gärdenfors, P. (2000). Conceptual Spaces: The Geometry of Thought. MIT Press.
📚 Gärdenfors, P. & Warglien, M. (2012). 'Semantics, conceptual spaces, and the meeting of minds.' Synthese.
📚 Gärdenfors, P. (2025). 'The Geometry and Dynamics of Meaning.' Topics in Cognitive Science 17(1):34-56.
📚 Clausner, T. & Croft, W. (1999). 'Domains and Image Schemas.' Cognitive Linguistics 10(1):1-31.
📚 Schank, R. (1972). 'Conceptual dependency: A theory of natural language understanding.' Cognitive Psychology 3(4):552-631.
📚 Balogh, P. (2026). 'Do Neurons Dream of Primitive Operators?' arXiv:2603.25975.
